title-group.article-title: Seroprevalence of Brucellosis Among Camels and Camel Owners in Ethiopia: A Systematic Review and Meta‐Analysis (2000–2025)

aff.named-content: College of Agriculture and Environmental Science
Department of Veterinary Epidemiology and Public Health

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2026 The Author(s). Veterinary Medicine and Science published by John Wiley & Sons Ltd.

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the terms of the http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/ License, which permits use, distribution and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited and is not used for commercial purposes.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Camel brucellosis is a neglected zoonotic disease that poses a significant economic and public health threat, particularly in regions where camel is vital to the pastoral community. Despite being an important health issue, there is limited comprehensive evidence on the burden of brucellosis in camel and camel owners in endemic countries. The objective of this review was to evaluate the prevalence of brucellosis among camels and camel owners in Ethiopia.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: The review sought major databases, including PubMed, ScienceDirect, Scopus, Web of Science and Google Scholar following the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta‐Analyses (PRISMA) protocols guidelines, with all searches completed by 30 September 2025. Heterogeneity among studies was assessed and a random‐effects model was used to estimate the pooled seroprevalence of brucellosis in camels and their owners. Subgroup analysis, meta‐regression and sensitivity analysis were used to identify the sources of variation. Funnel plots, along with Egger's tests, were employed to evaluate publication bias.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: The pooled seroprevalence of brucellosis in camel was found to be 3.0% (95% confidence interval [CI]: 2.0, 4.0), whereas in camel owners it was 14.0% (95% CI: 0.0, 34.0). The subgroup analysis revealed the highest seroprevalence of camel brucellosis was 5.0% (95% CI: 3.0, 6.0) in the Afar region, followed by 3.0% (95% CI: 2.0, 6.0) in Tigray and 2.0% (95% CI: 1.0, 3.0) in the Somali and Oromia regions. Furthermore, the analysis by publication year revealed that the seroprevalence from 2017 to 2025 was higher, at 3.0% (95% CI: 2.0, 4.0), compared to 2000–2016, which had a seroprevalence of 2.0% (95% CI: 1.0, 3.0).

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: This meta‐analysis emphasizes that brucellosis in camels and their owners continues to pose a significant threat in Ethiopia. Therefore, it is critical for veterinary and public health sectors to collaborate in designing and implementing effective prevention and control strategies for camel and camel owners’ brucellosis using a One Health framework.

abstract.p: • This systematic review and meta‐analysis included 45 articles, 39 focused on camels and 6 on camel farmers.
• A total of 23,930 study participants were assessed, comprising 22,362 camels and 1568 camel farmers.
• The overall pooled seroprevalence of brucellosis among camels and camel farmers was found to be 5.0%.
• Subgroup analysis revealed that the seroprevalence of brucellosis was 3.0% among camels and 14.0% among camel farmers, respectively.

def.p: Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta‐Analyses Protocols
International Prospective Register of Systematic Reviews

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Brucellosis is an infectious disease caused by Gram‐negative intracellular bacteria belonging to the genus Brucella (Seleem et al. 2010). The disease is distributed globally and affects human, wide range of wild animals and economically important domestic animals, including cattle, goats, sheep, camels and pigs (WHO 2020). The Brucella species of particular concern for zoonotic transmission are Brucella melitensis, Brucella abortus and Brucella suis (Seleem et al. 2010).

Camel brucellosis is found worldwide in regions where camel raising is practiced, with reported cases in different parts of Africa, Asia, Latin America and the Middle East (Dadar et al. 2022). Camel brucellosis is a neglected zoonotic disease that significantly impacts camelids, reducing reproductive efficiency and increasing mortality through abortion and decreased herd fertility in areas reliant on camels (Wario et al. 2024).

The transmission of brucellosis in camel can occur through inhalation, skin wounds, intact skin, the conjunctiva and venereal contact (Seleem et al. 2010). Humans can become infected through direct exposure to foetal tissues, vaginal secretions and afterbirth from infected animals, as well as by consuming raw or inadequately cooked meat and unpasteurized dairy products (Tabak et al. 2008). Camel brucellosis, which results from infections with B. abortus and B. melitensis, is a significant zoonotic disease that negatively impacts dairy and meat production (Dadar et al. 2022).

Brucellosis affects approximately 2.1 million humans globally each year (Laine et al. 2023), particularly in rural areas where people rely on livestock for their livelihoods and nutrition (Ambaw et al. 2025). The global burden of camel brucellosis is 9.23%, with the lowest prevalence reported in Oman at 0.34% (Dadar et al. 2022) and the highest observed in Sudan at 17.00% (Mohammed et al. 2023). In Ethiopia, camel brucellosis is endemic, with a seroprevalence of 4.30% (Dagnaw et al. 2024). The global prevalence of human brucellosis is also reported at 15.49% (Sherasiya 2024), with the lowest prevalence found in Brazil at 0.64% (Gonçalves et al. 2017) and the highest prevalence reported in Palestine at 76.00% (Alzuheir et al. 2022).

Although brucellosis has been effectively controlled in many developed nations, it continues to present significant socio‐economic, animal health and public health challenges in developing countries like Ethiopia. In these regions, large rural populations heavily depend on livestock for their livelihoods and food security (Ducrotoy et al. 2014). The persistence of brucellosis in these areas can be attributed to insufficient resources and the lack of coordinated control programs (Tschopp et al. 2021). Understanding the epidemiology and control measures of camel brucellosis is crucial for mitigating its effects on both animal populations and the livelihoods of those who depend on these animals (Wario et al. 2024).

Despite its significant economic and public health implications over the past few decades, there is limited comprehensive evidence regarding the national pooled prevalence of brucellosis in camels and camel owners in Ethiopia. This gap emphasizes the need for further study to develop effective prevention and control strategies for camel brucellosis among camels and camel owners. To address these issues, a systematic review and meta‐analysis were conducted on studies published between 2000 and 30 September 2025. This study aimed to estimate the pooled national seroprevalence of brucellosis in camels and camel owners in Ethiopia, while also identifying the most significant contributing factors in camel. The findings will support disease control strategies, inform veterinary and public health policymakers and highlight the zoonotic significance of brucellosis in camels and camel owners within the One Health approach.

sec.title: Study Protocols
sec.p: This systematic review and meta‐analysis was organized using the Condition, Context, and Population (CoCoPop) approach (Munn et al. 2015). It complied with the standards established by the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta‐Analyses (PRISMA) protocols 2020 (Parums 2021) to ensure a comprehensive approach to develop, execute and report the protocols. Prior to beginning data collection, the study protocol was registered with PROSPERO (International Prospective Register of Systematic Reviews) on 20 September 2025 with registration ID: CRD420251151909. The focus of the study was to estimates the seroprevalence of brucellosis in camels and camel owners, along with risk factors related to camel brucellosis in Ethiopia from 2000 to 30 September 2025.

sec.title: Inclusion and Exclusion Criteria
sec.p: The present study systematically explored all cross‐sectional research articles published in peer‐reviewed journals about the seroprevalence of camels and camel owners’ brucellosis in Ethiopia. To ensure the incorporation of the most recent and pertinent evidence, only studies published between 2000 and 30 September 2025 were considered. The scope of the review includes peer‐reviewed journals, published in English that reported on the seroprevalence of camels and camel owners’ brucellosis. Exclusion criteria for the research articles include lack of sufficient information or the presence of missing outcomes, studies focused on knowledge, attitudes and practices concerning camel brucellosis, personal opinions, conference proceedings and review articles.

sec.title: Literature Search Strategy
sec.p: For this systematic review and meta‐analysis, data were collected from five prominent academic databases, including PubMed, Scopus, Google Scholar, ScienceDirect and Web of Science. To collect all pertinent articles from the existing databases, MeSH terms and keywords associated with the study population were applied with Boolean operators (OR, AND) to combine search terms properly. The literature search strategy, prepared for PubMed database initially, was modified to use in other databases and comprised the following structure: ((Brucellosis [MeSH Terms] OR Brucella OR ‘Brucella abortus’ OR ‘Brucella melitensis’ OR ‘Brucella suis’) [MeSH Terms] AND (Camel OR Animals) AND (‘Camel owners’ OR ‘Camel herders’) OR Humans [MeSH Terms] AND (prevalence OR ‘risk factors’ OR epidemiology) AND Ethiopia [MeSH Terms]).

All full‐text retrieved articles were imported and organized using EndNote software version 20, and duplicate records were removed through a manual review process by titles and abstracts. Further studies were identified manually by cross‐referencing eligible studies, and a literature search was completed on 30 September 2025.

sec.title: Assessment of Study Quality and Risk of Bias
sec.p: The studies included in this systematic review and meta‐analysis were evaluated for quality using the critical appraisal tool developed by the Joanna Briggs Institute (JBI), which is tailored for prevalence studies (Munn et al. 2015). This tool comprises nine items, each of which is rated with one of four possible responses: yes, no, unclear or not applicable. The evaluation process was carried out by two independent teams: Team One, made up of SAK, AYA and AMW and Team Two, which included YGA, ATW and SGE. Both teams employed a four‐tier rating system of ‘yes’, ‘no’, ‘unclear’ and ‘not applicable’ to assess the quality of the articles. Any differences in evaluation results were addressed through discussions within the teams and consultations with a panel of expert researchers. In this scoring framework, responses categorized as ‘yes’ or ‘not applicable’ received a score of 1, whereas responses of ‘no’ or ‘unclear’ (including cases where information was either absent or considered irrelevant) received 0 points. The total scores for each article were aggregated on a scale from 0 to 9. According to these scores, the articles were classified into three quality categories: good (7–9 points), medium (4–6 points) and low (0–3 points). Notably, in this systematic review and meta‐analysis, all 45 articles included in the final analysis achieved a minimum score of four points, indicating that they possessed high or moderate quality.

sec.title: Data Extraction Among Eligible Studies
sec.p: The initial search results underwent a relevance screening process, allowing only studies that met the predefined inclusion criteria to advance the analysis phase. A standardized data extraction template was used for collecting information, carried out independently by two research teams: Team One (SAK, AYA and AMW) and Team Two (YGA, ATW and SGE). Any discrepancies in the collected data were resolved through discussions among the teams. The data extraction template included essential study parameters such as the author's name, diagnostic tests, year of publication, geographic region or location, sample size, number of events and prevalence of camels and their owners’ brucellosis. All collected information was carefully organized and cross‐verified using Microsoft Excel spreadsheet version 13 to ensure accuracy and consistency throughout the review process.

sec.title: Data Synthesis and Statistical Analysis
sec.p: The retrieved articles were assessed for eligibility by reviewing the complete text, and the necessary variables for analysis were documented. The findings of the study were presented through tables and forest plots. The data analysis was carried out using R programming language version 4.5.1 (R Core Team 2025). The pooled seroprevalence of brucellosis in camels and camel owners was determined using the ‘Meta’ package in R. Variance estimation was performed using the DerSimonian and Laird method, with a random‐effects meta‐analysis applied to account for variations among the studies (DerSimonian and Laird 2015). A forest plot was employed to visually summarize the prevalence of brucellosis in camels and camel owners, illustrating the 95% CI related to data from multiple studies. The Cochrane Q test was used to evaluate methodological differences among these studies (Cochran 1954), alongside the I 2 statistic, which indicates the fraction of total variability linked to heterogeneity (Higgins and Thompson 2002), and CI to estimate the possible range of true effects (IntHout et al. 2016). To explore sources of variability, subgroup analyses were carried out based on geographic regions, diagnostic tests, sample sizes of the studies, species and years of publication. A sensitivity analysis was conducted by systematically excluding one study at a time to evaluate the robustness of the overall evidence. Factors, such as diagnostic test and geographic region, were examined using univariable and multivariable meta‐regression models within a random‐effects framework to assess their influence on variability. To assess publication bias, a funnel plot asymmetry and Egger's regression analysis were employed (Egger et al. 1997).

sec.title: Selection and Identification of Studies
sec.p: In the preliminary stage of the systematic review process, a total of 273 studies were evaluated for their relevance to the research inquiry. After this initial evaluation, 81 studies were discarded due to duplication. Furthermore, 133 studies were removed following a comprehensive examination of their titles and abstracts, as they did not fulfil the inclusion criteria. Additionally, 12 studies were excluded for various reasons, such as incomplete outcome reporting, studies reported outside Ethiopia, non‐retrieved articles and publication dates prior to 2000. At the end, 45 studies (39 for camels and 6 for camel owners) were incorporated into the final systematic review and meta‐analysis, as depicted in the PRISMA flow diagram (Figure 1).

caption.p: Study selection using the PRISMA flow diagram for a systematic review and meta‐analysis of camels and camel owners in Ethiopia.

sec.title: Description of the Included Studies
sec.p: This systematic review and meta‐analysis examined a total of 45 studies, which included 39 studies focused on camels and 6 studies involving camel owners conducted between 2000 and 2025. A total of 22,362 camels were assessed, of which 634 tested positive for camel brucellosis. This indicates a seroprevalence rate of 2.84%. Additionally, among 1568 evaluated camel owners, 384 were found positive for brucellosis, resulting in a seroprevalence rate of 22.19%. The majority of retrieved studies were found in the Oromia region (17 studies) followed by the Somali (14 studies) and Afar regions (12 studies), which account for the majority of reports on the prevalence of camel brucellosis in Ethiopia during the specified time frame. Regarding diagnostic tests, most studies employed complement fixation test (CFT) (26 studies), whereas 19 studies used the indirect enzyme‐linked immunosorbent assay (I‐ELISA). The sample sizes for the included studies varied from 117 to 1850 camels, with observed prevalence rates of camel brucellosis in these regions ranging from 0.0% to 12.0% (Table 1).

caption.p: Prevalence of camels and camel owners brucellosis and overview of incorporated studies in Ethiopia, 2025 (n = 45).

fn.p: Abbreviations: CFT, complement fixation test; CI, confidence interval; I‐ELISA, indirect enzyme‐linked immunosorbent assay.

sec.title: Pooled Seroprevalence of Camel Brucellosis in Ethiopia
sec.p: The apparent seroprevalence of camel brucellosis ranged from 0.0% to 12.0%. The total pooled national seroprevalence of camel brucellosis was found to be 3.0% (95% CI: 2.0, 4.0). There was a significant variability in the seroprevalence rates of brucellosis across different studies (I 2 = 89.2%, Q (38) = 350.88, τ 2 = 0.0005, p < 0.0001). As a result, a random effects model was employed to determine the overall seroprevalence of camel brucellosis. Despite the significant heterogeneity observed among the individual studies, they were generally assigned equal weight, with each study contributions ranging from 1.4% to 3.0% (Figure 2). Additionally, the 95% predicted seroprevalence of camel brucellosis in Ethiopia is expected to fall within the range of 0.0% to 7.0%, indicating the future studies will likely report results within these intervals (Figure 2).

caption.p: Forest plot for pooled seroprevalence of camel brucellosis in Ethiopia. CI, confidence interval.

sec.title: Pooled Seroprevalence of Camel Owners’ Brucellosis in Ethiopia
sec.p: The apparent seroprevalence of camel owners’ brucellosis varies from 0.0% to 45.0% in Ethiopia. The findings from the meta‐analysis showed significant variability among the included studies (I 2 = 99.1%, Q (5) = 528.71, τ 2 = 0.0357, p < 0.0001). Hence, a random effects model was employed to estimate the overall seroprevalence of brucellosis among camel owners in Ethiopia. According to this model, the estimated pooled national seroprevalence of brucellosis in camel owners was 14.0% (95% CI: 0.0, 34.0). Furthermore, the 95% prediction interval is fluctuating from 0.0% to 66.0%, representing the expected range for future studies will likely be documented in the same study population in Ethiopia (Figure 3).

caption.p: Forest plot for pooled seroprevalence of camel owners’ brucellosis in Ethiopia. CI, confidence interval.

sec.title: Handling Heterogeneity
sec.p: The meta‐analysis in camel revealed a statistically significant variation among individual studies (Q = 350.88, τ 2 = 0.0005, I 2 = 89.2%, p < 0.001). To explore the sources of heterogeneity observed in the combined estimate from the random‐effects model, several methods were employed, including sensitivity analysis, subgroup analysis and meta‐regression analysis. These methods help to identify potential factors contributing to heterogeneity and enhance our understanding of the reliability of the results.

sec.title: Subgroup Analysis in Both Camels and Camel Owners
sec.p: A subgroup analysis based on species of the study population demonstrates that the highest seroprevalence of brucellosis was documented in camel owners at 14.0% (95% CI: 0.0, 34.0), whereas the lowest was in camels at 3.0% (95% CI: 2.0, 4.0). This result revealed the presence of a significant variability among individual studies included in camel and camel owners. However, this result of heterogeneity in the subgroup analysis was not significant between species (χ 2 = 2.01, df = 1, p = 0.1562) (Figure 4). The total pooled seroprevalence of brucellosis among the 45 included studies in camels and their owners was found to be 5.0% (95% CI: 2.0, 7.0) with 2.0% to 7.0% chance of feature studies (Figure 4).

caption.p: Forest plot for subgroup analysis of brucellosis by species, Ethiopia.

sec.title: Subgroup Analysis of Camel Brucellosis
sec.p: A subgroup analysis in camel population was also conducted based on various factors, including geographical regions, diagnostic tests, sample size categories and publication years. Significant differences in the prevalence of camel brucellosis were observed across different regions. The subgroup analysis of brucellosis in camels indicated that animals from the Afar region had the highest infection rates at 5.0% (95% CI: 3.0, 6.0%), whereas the lowest was found in Somali region 2.0% (95% CI: 1.0–3.0) (Table 2). The rates of camel brucellosis ranged from 2.0% (95% CI: 2.0, 3.0) as detected by the CFT to 4.0% (95% CI: 2.0, 6.0) according to the I‐ELISA test (Table 2).

caption.p: Subgroup analysis of camel brucellosis based on different risk factors, Ethiopia.

fn.p: Abbreviations: CFT, complement fixation test; I‐ELISA, indirect enzyme‐linked immunosorbent assay.

sec.title: Sensitivity Analysis
sec.p: A sensitivity analysis using a leave‐one‐out approach was conducted to evaluate the impact of each study on the overall effect size estimate. The combined meta‐analysis yielded a pooled effect size of 3.0%, which was consistent with the CI of individual included studies. The sensitivity testing also confirmed the robustness of these results; excluding any one study from the analysis did not significantly alter the overall prevalence estimate (Figure 5). Therefore, the meta‐analysis result that included all studies in this investigation was deemed reliable.

caption.p: Sensitivity analysis on the prevalence of camel brucellosis in Ethiopia. CI, confidence interval.

sec.title: Influence Analysis
sec.p: The Baujat diagnostic plot was employed to identify the studies that significantly influence the heterogeneity observed in this meta‐analysis. Notably, the research conducted by Abebe et al. (2014) and Gessese et al. (2014) was found to have a significant effect on the overall heterogeneity in our meta‐analysis (Figure 6).

caption.p: Baujat diagnostic plot for camel brucellosis in Ethiopia.

sec.title: Meta‐Regression Models
sec.p: To pinpoint the sources of data variability in this analysis, we implemented both univariable and multivariable meta‐regression models. In the univariable analysis, publication year and sample size were regarded as continuous variables, whereas study region and diagnostic tests were considered categorical variables. We examined the linear relationships of these variables with the effect sizes, which were the outcome of interest. Notably, geographic region was exhibited significant correlation with variability among the studies. Of these factors, geographic region was determined to have the greatest impact, accounting for 33.92% of the total heterogeneity (R 2 = 33.92%). Additionally, the multivariable model further explaining 40.59% of the heterogeneity (R 2 = 40.59%). The regression coefficients from the multivariable analysis indicated that studies found in Afar region (β = 0.0264, p = 0.001) was linked to a higher effect sizes compared to studies found in Oromia region, after adjustments for other moderators (Table 3).

caption.p: Univariable and multivariable meta‐regression analysis on the prevalence of camel brucellosis in Ethiopia.

fn.p: Abbreviations: CFT, complement fixation test; CI, confidence interval; I‐ELISA, indirect enzyme‐linked immunosorbent assay.

sec.title: Trends of Camel Brucellosis Over Time
sec.p: In Ethiopia, the seroprevalence of camel brucellosis exhibited a gradual increase from 2000 to 2025. However, this observed increment (the fitted regression line) was not statistically significant, as indicated by the results (β = 0.0018, t = 0905, p = 0.371). Additionally, the plot reveals the presence of some outlier studies published between 2000 and 2025 (Figure 7).

caption.p: Trends in camel brucellosis seroprevalence over time (2000–2025).

sec.title: Publication Bias
sec.p: Publication bias was evaluated using a combination of visual and statistical approaches. Graphically, the funnel plot revealed an asymmetrical distribution of effect size estimates across the included studies (Figure 8). To support this visual observation, Egger's regression analysis revealed a coefficient of 4.58 (z = 8.36; p < 0.0001). The analysis indicated a statistically significant asymmetry in the aggregated data, providing strong evidence of publication bias or small‐study effects that may have influence on the meta‐analysis results.

caption.p: Funnel plot showing the pooled seroprevalence of camel brucellosis in Ethiopia.

sec.p: To address the issue of publication bias for camel brucellosis, we conducted a trim and fill analysis, which resulted in the imputation of 18 hypothetical studies on the left side of the funnel plot (Figure 9). As a result, the overall pooled seroprevalence of camel brucellosis in Ethiopia, based on a total of 57 studies (39 observed and 18 imputed), was determined to be 1.16%, with a 95% CI of (0.11, 2.20) and 95% prediction intervals ranging from 0.00 to 8.52.

caption.p: Trim and fill analysis using imputation of 18 hypothetical studies.

sec.title: Association of Sex and the Seroprevalence of Camel Brucellosis
sec.p: In this meta‐analysis, 39 studies were included, of which 21 examined the relationship between the seroprevalence of camel brucellosis and sex. Among these, 2 studies found a significant association between the sex of camels and the seroprevalence of camel brucellosis (Edema et al. 2025; Asmelash et al. 2025). However, the other 19 studies reported no significant findings. The pooled analysis of these 21 studies demonstrated a significant overall association between the sex of camels and the seroprevalence of brucellosis (OR = 1.59, 95% CI: 1.19, 2.14) (Figure 10). This suggests that female camels are 59% more likely to develop camel brucellosis compared to male camels.

caption.p: Forest plot for pooled odds ratio of sex and seroprevalence of camel brucellosis in Ethiopia. CI, confidence interval.

sec.title: Association of Body Condition and the Seroprevalence of Camel Brucellosis
sec.p: Among the 39 studies reviewed, 9 specifically investigated the relationship between body condition score and the prevalence of camel brucellosis. None of these studies found a statistically significant difference in brucellosis prevalence between camels in poor body condition and those in good body condition (Figure 11). The overall effect size was 1.91 (OR = 1.91, 95% CI: 1.14, 3.20), indicating that individuals in poor body condition were 1.91 times more likely to be infected than those in good body condition. The pooled analysis did reveal a significant association between poor body condition and the seroprevalence of camel brucellosis.

caption.p: Forest plot for pooled odds ratio of poor versus good body condition score and seroprevalence of camel brucellosis in Ethiopia. CI, confidence interval.

sec.p: On the other hand, among the nine studies that examined the relationship between medium against good body condition and the seroprevalence of brucellosis in camels, all studies revealed a non‐significant association (Figure 11). The pooled estimated effect size for body condition score was (OR = 1.06, 95% CI: 0.60, 1.90) (Figure 12). This indicates that camels in medium body condition had slightly higher, but not statistically significant, odds of brucellosis compared to those in good body condition.

caption.p: Forest plot for pooled odds ratio of medium against good body condition and seroprevalence of camel brucellosis in Ethiopia. CI, confidence interval.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: Camel brucellosis is a neglected zoonotic disease that poses a significant economic and public health threat, especially in areas where camels play a crucial role in the pastoral society. This disease results in reduced milk production, reproductive failures, arthritis and financial losses for camel farmers (Abdel‐Rady et al. 2025). This systematic review and meta‐analysis assess the seroprevalence of brucellosis in camels and camel owners in Ethiopia. The aim is to provide comprehensive evidence for developing effective prevention and control strategies, ultimately reducing the public health burden and benefiting pastoral economies and community well‐being. The findings will support stakeholders in implementing targeted interventions.

In the current meta‐analysis, the pooled seroprevalence of camel brucellosis was found 3.0% (95% CI: 2.0, 4.0). This finding is consistent with previous reports in Ethiopia and elsewhere, which noted seroprevalence rates of 2.6% (Tadesse 2016), 3.0% (Sibhat et al. 2022), 3.8% in Saudi Arabia (Almuzaini et al. 2025) and 3.4% in Algeria (Kaaboub et al. 2025). This closeness in seroprevalence may be related to similar agroecology, camel management, the employed diagnostic tests and animal health services (Hussen et al. 2023).

On the other hand, this result was lower than other reports with 4.3% (Dagnaw et al. 2024) in Ethiopia, 20.9% in Nigeria (Akinyemi et al. 2022), 17.0% in Sudan (Mohammed et al. 2023), 20.6% in Qatar (Alhussain et al. 2022), 14.5% in Iran (Alamian and Dadar 2019), 14.0% in Libya (Ahmed et al. 2010) and 9.2% in the world meta‐analysis (Dadar et al. 2022). The difference in seroprevalence of camel brucellosis may be attributed to variations in camel management and environmental conditions (Ambaw et al. 2025). Besides, the annual rainfall is very low in the pastoral camel rearing areas of Ethiopia, leading to a scarcity of water and feed. As a result, camels frequently migrate in search of feed and water, which increases the likelihood of direct or indirect contact between animals and may increase the risk of brucellosis transmission. Additionally, there is no vaccination program for camel brucellosis in Ethiopia, which may inflate seroprevalence and distribution of camel brucellosis in advance (Ahmed 2021). In the camel‐rearing regions of Ethiopia, the combination of pastoralism and communal grazing, along with limited access to veterinary services and policies, augments the risk of camel brucellosis becoming endemic in Ethiopia. Factors contributing to this issue include sharing grazing pastures with other animal species, lack of awareness about camel brucellosis, cohabitation with other ruminants, unreported outbreaks and the absence of effective brucellosis control programs (Dadar et al. 2022; Racloz et al. 2013). On the other hand, the confirmatory serological diagnostic tests employed in the included studies were either I‐ELISA or CFT, which may contribute to the variations in seroprevalence of camel brucellosis (Hussen et al. 2023).

The seroprevalence of camel brucellosis is also higher in Ethiopia's bordering countries, including 18.3% in Egypt (Hussein et al. 2025), 17.0% in Sudan (Mohammed et al. 2023), 20.0% in Kenya (Lokamar et al. 2022) and 7% in Somalia (Mohamud et al. 2021). This elevated seroprevalence in neighbouring countries may be attributed to limitation in developing and implementing effective transboundary veterinary and public health strategies, inadequate veterinary services and inefficient disease surveillance and control measures (Hikal et al. 2023). Furthermore, efforts to reduce brucellosis in Ethiopia have been hampered by policymakers’ inattention and the prevalence of illegal livestock trade with neighbouring countries. In East Africa, over 95% of cross‐border trade occurs through unofficial routes, with the illicit movement of livestock from Ethiopia to Kenya, Djibouti and Somalia generating between 250 and 300 million dollars annually (Pavanello 2010). This illegal trade contributes to the spread of animal diseases across borders. However, the association between the occurrence of brucellosis and illegal animal trade has not still explored (Hikal et al. 2023). To mitigate the impact of brucellosis on both animals and humans, it is recommended that East African countries adopt and enforce the existing One Health policies as proposed by WOAH (Ambaw et al. 2025).

Camel can carry the bacteria that cause brucellosis, which can be transmitted to humans through the consumption of raw milk or through direct contact with infected animals. Hence, individuals at the highest risk of exposure, including veterinarians, camel herders, butchers and abattoir workers, may be contracted to the diseases (Almuzaini et al. 2025).

The pooled seroprevalence of brucellosis among camel owners in Ethiopia was 14.0%, 95% CI [0.00–34.0]. This result was in agreement with previous reports in Ethiopia 14.0% (Asrie et al. 2025), in Nigeria 17.6% (Akinyemi et al. 2022), in the world 15.49% (Sherasiya 2024), 15.53% (Khoshnood et al. 2022), in the Middle East region 20.5% (Dadar et al. 2024) and 17.3% (Musallam et al. 2016). On the other hand, the current finding was higher than the existed studies 5.0% (Sibhat et al. 2022) and 6.9% (Dagnaw et al. 2024) in Ethiopia and 0.6% in Brazil (Gonçalves et al. 2017) and 8.0% in Saudi Arabia (Almuzaini et al. 2025). The current finding is also lower than reports in Libya, 40.0% (Ahmed et al. 2010), and Palestine, 76.0% (Alzuheir et al. 2022). This inconsistency among seroprevalence of brucellosis in camel owners’ across different studies can be attributed to factors such as environmental conditions, study populations, farming systems and sample sizes. Although many reports are limited in geographic scope or focus on specific agro‐ecologies, these findings indicate that brucellosis in camels and camel owners may be a widespread problem in Ethiopia (Dagnaw et al. 2024).

The persistence of brucellosis in developing countries like Ethiopia is influenced by several risk factors. One significant issue is the lack of coordinated efforts among policymakers, health officials, veterinary sectors and farmers, which undermines effective control and prevention strategies. Other contributing factors include inadequate infrastructure for disease surveillance, limited access to diagnostic facilities and lack of public awareness and education about the disease. Additionally, socio‐economic challenges such as poverty and insufficient resources for healthcare and veterinary services worsen the situation, allowing brucellosis to remain endemic in these regions (Hikal et al. 2023). In addition, the main contributing factors for human brucellosis are the consumption of contaminated milk and dairy products, as well as close contact with animals. It has been found that a combination of high‐risk behaviours, a lack of understanding of the disease and insufficient prevention efforts contribute to the persistence of brucellosis in developing countries (Musallam et al. 2016).

Subgroup analysis revealed that camel brucellosis is most prevalent in the Afar region, followed by Oromia and Tigray. The Afar region consists primarily of low‐altitude drylands and is predominantly inhabited by pastoral communities. These findings underscore the influence of environmental and geographical factors on the transmission of camel brucellosis, highlighting the need for control strategies that are tailored to specific regions. Additionally, lack of awareness among pastoral communities about zoonotic transmission, close contact with infected animals and the consumption of raw milk contribute to the persistence and spread of camel brucellosis. The extensive pastoral lifestyle, characterized by high animal movement and the mixing of herds, further facilitates transmission. Furthermore, occupational exposure and unsafe handling of dystocia cases in animals increase the risk for both camels and human brucellosis in the Afar region (Wegi et al. 2021).

This regional variation in seroprevalence of camel brucellosis may be due to differences in the number of studies conducted, geographical locations, climate factors and management practices (Dadar et al. 2022). Additionally, significant progress in preventing and controlling brucellosis has been made through comprehensive measures that focus on understanding the sources of infection, modes of transmission and susceptible hosts (Khoshnood et al. 2022). Besides, evidence indicated that the consumption of raw milk is noticeably high in camel‐rearing pastoral areas of Ethiopia. This practice raises the risk of brucellosis transmission from animals to humans (Tschopp et al. 2021). This trend may be related to the cultural beliefs of pastoral societies, who often think that raw camel milk tastes better and is associated with various health benefits, including aphrodisiac properties (Njenga et al. 2020).

To effectively address the issue of brucellosis in camels and humans, it is crucial to implement various targeted interventions. These include public awareness campaigns, the use of protective clothing for handling aborted camels and the pasteurization of milk. Additional measures should involve camel vaccination, proper hygiene practices, quarantine protocols and serological testing to identify infected animals. It is also essential to strengthen collaboration between public health and veterinary sectors as a One Health framework (Almuzaini et al. 2025).

In this meta‐analysis female camels are more likely to be seropositive for brucellosis than males. This observation is consistent with reports from various regions in Ethiopia (Ambaw et al. 2025; Asmelash et al. 2025), in Qatar (Alhussain et al. 2022) and in Saudi Arabia (Almuzaini et al. 2025). However, male camel was also more infected to brucellosis in some reports (Edema et al. 2025; Mohammed et al. 2023). The increased seroprevalence of brucellosis in female camel may be linked to erythritol, a sugar alcohol found in high concentrations in the placenta and foetal fluids of pregnant animals. This condition creates an environment that promotes the growth and multiplication of Brucella organisms. As females have higher concentrations of erythritol than males, they may be more susceptible to brucellosis (Radostits et al. 2007). Furthermore, the higher seroprevalence of brucellosis in female camels could also be related to age. Mostly, male camels are sold at a younger age for meat production, resulting in a larger population of younger males in the camel herd compared to adult males. When young animals become infected with Brucella, the bacteria typically localize in the regional lymph nodes without triggering antibody production until the animal reaches sexual maturity and produces sex hormones that promote the growth of Brucella (Radostits et al. 2007). Moreover, pastoral farmers usually contain more female camels than males. Females are retained for a longer time for milking, which increases their exposure to brucellosis. The higher infection rates among female camel can further increase the risk of disease transmission to humans, particularly among pastoralists (camel farmers) who often consume raw milk and meat from these animals (Tschopp et al. 2021).

The seroprevalence of camel brucellosis was found to be higher in camel with poor body condition scores compared to those in good body condition. This suggests that being poor body condition is linked to higher infection rates. The result is in line with (Wegi et al. 2021: Abebe et al. 2017; Radostits et al. 2007). On the other hand, some studies have reported higher seropositivity in camel with good or medium body condition than in those with poor body condition (Edema et al. 2025), although these variations were not statistically significant. This discrepancy could be attributed to the sampling distribution, as most camels in these studies were in good body condition, which may have skewed the results. The relation between poor body condition and higher seroprevalence is biologically plausible, as underfed animals typically exhibit reduced immune function, making them more susceptible to infections such as camel brucellosis (Jara et al. 2020). Poor nutrition may also reduce the production and function of immune cells and antibodies, which decreases the camel's ability to effectively defend against pathogens like Brucella (Radostits et al. 2007).

sec.title: Limitations
sec.p: This meta‐analysis has several limitations. The first limitation was lack of studies from a certain regions, which may result in biased pooled national seroprevalence estimates of brucellosis in camel and camel owners. Some factors contributing to the seroprevalence of brucellosis were not included in our study due to insufficient data. Variations in ecological and camel management practices may not be adequately represented. Excluding studies published in non‐English languages may also represent another limitation. Additionally, nearly all studies relied on serological tests such as CFT and I‐ELISA, which can yield false‐negative or false‐positive results, potentially not reflecting the true burden of brucellosis among camels and their owners. Furthermore, the studies included in this analysis cover the period from 2000 to 30 September 2025, which may overlook significant historical trends influenced by climate and communities behavioural changes.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: The pooled seroprevalence of brucellosis among camels and camel owners in Ethiopia is high. Living in the Afar region, being female, and having poor body condition are significant contributing factors for the seroprevalence of camel brucellosis. Therefore, veterinary and public health sectors, policymakers and research institutions should prioritize evidence‐based control programs that focus on high‐burden regions. It is essential to implement specific interventions tailored to female camel and improve access to animal health services to prevent and control brucellosis effectively. Large sample sizes in meta‐analyses can lead to statistically significant findings that cannot reflect meaningful clinical effects. Therefore, it is important to consider the context and characteristics of the primary studies when interpreting these results. Furthermore, additional research is necessary to assess the economic impact of camel brucellosis and evaluate the effectiveness of interventions aimed at combating this disease in Ethiopia.

sec.title: Author Contributions
sec.p: Yihenew Getahun Ambaw: conceptualization, investigation, methodology, data curation, formal analysis, writing – original draft. Simegnew Adugna Kallu: methodology, formal analysis, writing – review and editing. Ambachew Motbaynor Wubaye: methodology, formal analysis, writing – review and editing. Simachew Getaneh Endalamew: conceptualization, investigation, methodology, writing – original draft. Andnet Yirga Assefa: data curation, supervision, writing – review and editing. Alebachew Tilahun Wassie: data curation, supervision, writing – review and editing.

sec.title: Funding
sec.p: The authors have nothing to report.

sec.title: Ethics Statement
sec.p: The authors have nothing to report.

sec.title: Conflicts of Interest
sec.p: The authors declare no conflicts of interest.

ack.title: Acknowledgements
ack.p: The authors would like to acknowledge primary researchers for the compiled articles.

sec.title: Data Availability Statement
sec.p: The data are available on reasonable request from the corresponding author.
